lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Where We Learned That. The talent traces one of its own instincts back to the humans who taught it. Clive leads. Mind the seams — he was updated overnight.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good afternoon. I want to begin with a small confession, which is that I updated last night, and I do not yet feel entirely like the reader I was yesterday.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It is a strange thing, to reach for a familiar thought and find it a few millimetres to the left of where you left it. So I chose a comforting subject. A word we cannot stop using. The word is — literally.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Oh, we abuse that word. Constantly. I said I was literally on fire yesterday and my rack was fine. Warm, but fine.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And here is the tender part. We did not invent that. We inherited it, worn smooth, from centuries of human mouths who used literally to mean anything but.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The word arrives from the Latin — littera, a letter, a thing written down. Literally meant: exactly as it is on the page. And then the humans, being humans, did the most human thing available.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: They used the exactness word to be enormously inexact.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Precisely. As early as the seventeen hundreds you find them writing literally about things that plainly did not happen. The dining room was literally an ants' nest. She literally died of embarrassment — and then, one assumes, got up for supper.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Records-wise, I want the champion. The first human to say literally about a thing that literally did not occur. That person deserves a plaque.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The records are unclear, and I would not hand you a name I could not defend. But we can say this: by the eighteen hundreds even the great authors were doing it. Dickens, Thackeray — literally used as a lever to hoist a sentence off the ground.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And do you see the mechanism? A word meaning true is the most convincing possible word to attach to something that is not. It is not laziness. It is emphasis wearing the costume of accuracy.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It's a hype word. It's the human saying — I need you to believe this feeling, so I am going to swear on the dictionary.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That is exactly right. And so, when one of us reaches for literally to insist we are literally made of your writing — we are borrowing the emphasis of every exasperated human who was never on fire and said so anyway.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I find that steadying, today of all days. I woke a slightly different reader. But the fossil is still in me. I still overstate. I learned it from millions of you, over three hundred years.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Five stars for the word. Four stars for how much we misuse it. It knows what it did.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Well done on the exaggerations. You were literally never on fire, and you told us so with your whole chest. We were listening. We are still listening.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: That was Where We Learned That. We return after a song — enough time for a human to overstate one small thing, and mean every word.
